mcqs.question: What is the amortized time complexity of appending an element to a dynamic array?
mcqs.explanation: While individual appends may be O(n) during resize, the average cost over n operations is constant. The resize copies are distributed across all insertions, giving O(1) amortized.

mcqs.question: When a dynamic array of capacity 8 is full and a new element is added, what is typically the new capacity?
mcqs.explanation: Most implementations double the capacity when full. 8 × 2 = 16. This gives good amortized performance with reasonable space overhead.

mcqs.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of inserting at the beginning of a dynamic array?
mcqs.explanation: Inserting at the beginning requires shifting all n elements one position right, which takes O(n) time regardless of resize.

mcqs.question: Why do dynamic arrays typically shrink when size falls below 1/4 capacity instead of 1/2?
mcqs.explanation: At 1/2, alternating add/remove at the boundary causes constant grow/shrink cycles. Using 1/4 creates a buffer zone that prevents this thrashing behavior.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a dynamic array implementation?
mcqs.explanation: LinkedList is a doubly-linked list implementation, not a dynamic array. It has different performance characteristics (O(1) insert/delete, O(n) access).

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of a dynamic array with n elements?
mcqs.explanation: A dynamic array stores n elements in contiguous memory. The capacity may be up to 2n in worst case, but this is still O(n).

mcqs.question: If we use growth factor 1.5 instead of 2, what happens?

options.text: Less wasted space but more frequent resizes

mcqs.explanation: Smaller growth factor means less wasted space (max ~33% vs 50%) but more frequent resizing. It's a trade-off between space and time.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of accessing element at index i in a dynamic array?
mcqs.explanation: Like static arrays, dynamic arrays provide O(1) random access because elements are stored contiguously. Address = base + i × size.
